title: 南華早報：被制裁香港官員靠老婆銀行戶口出糧及消費

replies.msg: https://www.scmp.com/news/hong-kong/law-and-crime/article/3113698/wife-takes-charge-money-after-hong-kong-official-us

A senior Hong Kong official’s entire salary goes straight into his wife’s bank account every month. She handles all their household expenses, pays his mobile phone bill and picks up the tab when they shop or eat out, he told the Post.

He is among 29 current and retired Hong Kong and mainland China officials sanctioned by the United States over Beijing’s introduction of a national security law in the city in June. The individual spoke to the Post on condition of anonymity.
多謝你話俾全世界知

制裁埋家人啦咁要
資助恐怖分子
銀包人都要被制裁了
等老婆都制裁埋

The senior official who spoke anonymously to the Post said after he was forced to close his accounts, he transferred his life savings to his wife’s account.

Two days after his name appeared on the sanction list, an American financial institution in Hong Kong informed him in a letter that he no longer had access to his credit card. Then his banks contacted him, “advising” him to close his accounts.

During meetings with bank officials, it became clear to him that they were worried about possible US penalties if they did not stop doing business with him.

即係有人情願出現金都唔信個老公

It is ridiculous. We implement the law in Hong Kong to punish lawbreakers, but we are being sanctioned by another country

-Anonymous Hong Kong official

“Under the sanctions, they are also victims and they had no choice but to close my accounts,” he said.
Since then, his life has changed in numerous ways.
“Without a credit card or electronic payment account, I have to pay in cash or with an Octopus card when I shop or dine out, unless I go with my wife,” he said. “I have to rely on her in my daily life.”
Admitting that the inconvenience was a bother, he said: “It is ridiculous. We implement the law in Hong Kong to punish lawbreakers, but we are being sanctioned by another country.”

quote.msg:
The senior official who spoke anonymously to the Post said after he was forced to close his accounts, he transferred his life savings to his wife’s account.

Two days after his name appeared on the sanction list, an American financial institution in Hong Kong informed him in a letter that he no longer had access to his credit card. Then his banks contacted him, “advising” him to close his accounts.

During meetings with bank officials, it became clear to him that they were worried about possible US penalties if they did not stop doing business with him.

replies.msg: 係咪大導演

The Post has learned that affected officials have a choice of receiving their salary in cash, transferring it into the bank account of a family member or letting the government retain part of the money until they needed it.
A second official who confirmed that he no longer had banking services said he was in the same boat as city leader Lam, but declined to say if he also kept his cash at home.
A third official said his banks did not ask him to close his accounts immediately after the US sanctions were announced. “But they restricted some of my banking services so I could not do online banking, make overseas remittances or use my credit cards,” he said.
